title: Zscaler Cloud Firewall Rule Hit Analysis
spl:
index=firewall sourcetype="zscaler:fw"
| stats count by rule_label, action, dest_country
| sort -count
description: Cloud firewall hits extend NGFW-style control to roaming and branch users.
value: Cloud firewall hits extend NGFW-style control to roaming and branch users.
implementation: Use cloud firewall logs for non-web TCP/UDP. Identify noisy rules and geo anomalies.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Zscaler blocks from category misclassifications, new SaaS rollouts, or short policy-tightening windows; triage with app owners and DLP before assuming compromise.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_zscaler`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=zscaler:fw`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Use cloud firewall logs for non-web TCP/UDP. Identify noisy rules and geo anomalies.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=firewall sourcetype="zscaler:fw"
| stats count by rule_label, action, dest_country
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Zscaler Cloud Firewall Rule Hit Analysis** — Cloud firewall hits extend NGFW-style control to roaming and branch users.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=zscaler:fw`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_zscaler`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: firewall; **sourcetype**: zscaler:fw. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=firewall, sourcetype="zscaler:fw". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by rule_label, action, dest_country** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Zscaler Cloud Firewall Rule Hit Analysis** — Cloud firewall hits extend NGFW-style control to roaming and branch users.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=zscaler:fw`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_zscaler`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over a window that includes both normal production traffic and, if you have one, a documented test (vendor sandbox, lab rule hit, or exercise). Compare a few sample events to the vendor’s own console or reports and tune indexes, fields, and thresholds so alerts are actionable before paging.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (rules), Map (dest country).
grandmaExplanation: We use Zscaler data and alerts to stay ahead of the risks described in "Zscaler Cloud Firewall Rule Hit Analysis". We help the team notice trouble early, while a situation is still small and containable.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` dataset after normalizing this feed to the model; otherwise the `cimSpl` tstats search may return no rows.
